content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_d31cdae3

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write. Transcript contains durable user travel history/interests and a reusable trip-planning note with itinerary decisions and rationale.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-14: attended_event; The user took a solo backpacking trip to Europe last summer, spending two weeks backpacking through Italy, Switzerland, and Germany.; scope=User travel history from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- profile benchmark:longmemeval:gpt4_d31cdae3:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user is interested in science and technology.
- The user planned to visit Munich in late September for 3-4 nights.
- The user was thinking of visiting the Deutsches Museum while in Munich.
- The user has visited Munich before.
- The user is planning a trip to Germany with time in Munich.
- The user has previously visited Germany.
- The user was thinking of visiting the Dachau Concentration Camp Memorial Site while in Munich.
- The user was considering visiting the Oktoberfest grounds during the planned Munich trip.
- The user was thinking of visiting Nymphenburg Palace while in Munich.
- note hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-note-4: CONTEXT; User's prior Europe backpacking trip context; The user described a solo Europe backpacking trip from the previous summer lasting two weeks through Italy, Switzerland, and Germany, and said they were glad to have visited Munich during that trip. The transcript contains an assistant contradiction about whether Germany/Munich was visited, so later use should treat the user's direct statement as the stronger source but note the conflict if precision matters.; scope=User travel history context from a past-chat transcript, with an internal contradiction noted
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-15: has_experience_with; The user has visited Munich before.; scope=User travel history from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-5: has_goal; The user is planning a trip to Germany with time in Munich.; scope=User travel planning context from the 2023-05-23 imported session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-13: current_metric_value; The user's planned time allocation for the Deutsches Museum was at least half a day.; scope=User's planned Deutsches Museum visit during the Munich trip
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-8: has_goal; The user was considering visiting the Oktoberfest grounds during the planned Munich trip.; scope=User Munich trip interests from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-7: has_goal; The user planned to visit Munich in late September for 3-4 nights.; scope=User Munich trip plan from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-11: has_goal; The user was thinking of visiting the Deutsches Museum while in Munich.; scope=User Munich trip interests from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-9: has_goal; The user was thinking of visiting Nymphenburg Palace while in Munich.; scope=User Munich trip interests from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- claim hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-claim-10: has_goal; The user was thinking of visiting the Dachau Concentration Camp Memorial Site while in Munich.; scope=User Munich trip interests from the imported 2023-05-23 session
- note hot-path-7f9e69c03c05-note-2: CONTEXT; Munich trip planning context for late September; The user was planning a Munich, Germany visit in late September for 3–4 nights, with possible itinerary interests including Oktoberfest at Theresienwiese, Nymphenburg Palace, Dachau Concentration Camp Memorial Site, and Deutsches Museum. Advice in the session focused on practical transit, guided-tour options, and prioritizing major attractions rather than bookings.; scope=User travel-planning context for an upcoming Munich trip discussed in LongMemEval session 1

        - mention=I'm trying to plan a trip to Arizona; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<0>; type=OTHER; name=User's solo trip to Arizona; reason=The target message discusses a reusable planned trip entity not present among candidates.
- mention=I've been to the Grand Canyon before with my family on a road trip across the American Southwest; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<1>; type=OTHER; name=User's family road trip across the American Southwest; reason=First-person travel experience is a concrete reusable past trip entity and no matching candidate exists.

- mention=the Grand Canyon; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=LOCATION; name=Grand Canyon; reason=Grand Canyon is a concrete location referenced as previously visited and absent from candidates.

- mention=Navajo Tribal Park; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=LOCATION; name=Navajo Tribal Park; reason=Mentioned as a specific place associated with a guided tour; no candidate exists.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/23 (Tue) 00:24
Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan a trip to Arizona and I was wondering if you could recommend some must-see attractions and activities. By the way, I've been to the Grand Canyon before with my family on a road trip across the American Southwest a few years ago, but I'm looking to explore more of the state on my own this time.
assistant: Arizona has so much to offer beyond the Grand Canyon! I'd be happy to recommend some must-see attractions and activities for your solo trip. Here are a few suggestions:

**Nature and Outdoors**

1. **Sedona**: Known for its breathtaking red rock formations, Sedona is a hiker's paradise. Explore the scenic trails, take a Jeep tour, or simply soak up the vibrant energy of this artistic town.
2. **Monument Valley**: While you've seen the Grand Canyon, Monument Valley is another iconic Arizona landmark. Take a guided tour to explore the Navajo Tribal Park's stunning sandstone formations.
3. **Antelope Canyon**: A photographer's dream! This slot canyon near Page is a marvel of erosion, with surreal sandstone formations. Book a guided tour to explore its depths.
4. **Lake Powell**: Straddling the Arizona-Utah border, Lake Powell is a massive man-made lake with crystal-clear waters. Rent a boat, kayak, or take a guided tour to explore its many inlets and coves.

**Cities and Culture**

1. **Phoenix**: Arizona's capital city offers a rich cultural scene. Visit the Heard Museum to learn about Native American art and history, or explore the vibrant arts district in Roosevelt Row.
2. **Tucson**: This desert city is known for its Southwestern charm. Stroll through the historic adobe architecture of Downtown Tucson, visit the Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum, or explore the vibrant 4th Avenue arts district.
3. **Jerome**: This former mining town turned artist colony is perched on the side of Cleopatra Hill. Browse the shops, galleries, and restaurants, and enjoy the stunning views of the Verde Valley.

**Unique Experiences**

1. **Hot Air Expeditions**: Take a hot air balloon ride over the Sonoran Desert for a breathtaking sunrise view.
2. **Stargazing**: Arizona's clear skies make it an ideal spot for stargazing. Book a guided tour or visit one of the many dark sky preserves, such as the Oracle State Park.
3. **Native American Reservations**: Arizona is home to 22 Native American reservations. Visit the Navajo Nation, Hopi Tribe, or Tohono O'odham Nation to learn about their rich cultures and histories.

**Food and Drink**

1. **Southwestern Cuisine**: Try traditional dishes like tacos, burritos, and chimichangas at local eateries like Macayo's or BK Carne Asada.
2. **Craft Beer**: Arizona has a thriving craft beer scene. Visit breweries like Four Peaks Brewing Co. in Tempe or Borderlands Brewing Co. in Tucson.
3. **Prickly Pear Cactus Candy**: Indulge in this unique Arizona treat, made from the fruits of the prickly pear cactus.

These are just a few of the many amazing experiences Arizona has to offer. Depending on your interests and the time of year you visit, there are plenty of other attractions and activities to explore. Have a fantastic trip!
user: I'm really interested in exploring Sedona and Monument Valley, since I've already seen the Grand Canyon. Can you recommend any specific hiking trails in Sedona that are suitable for solo travelers?
assistant: Sedona and Monument Valley are both incredible destinations.

Sedona has numerous hiking trails for all skill levels, and many are suitable for solo travelers. Here are some popular and scenic trails to consider:

**Easy to Moderate Trails**

1. **Cathedral Rock Trail**: A 1.2-mile out-and-back trail that takes you to the base of Cathedral Rock, one of Sedona's most iconic formations. The trail is relatively flat and well-marked.
2. **Bell Rock Trail**: A 3.5-mile loop trail that offers stunning views of Bell Rock and the surrounding red rocks. The trail has some gentle inclines, but it's still accessible for most hikers.
3. **Boynton Canyon Trail**: A 6.1-mile out-and-back trail that takes you through a scenic canyon with towering red rock walls. The trail has some moderate inclines, but the scenery is well worth the effort.

**More Challenging Trails**

1. **Devil's Bridge Trail**: A 4.2-mile out-and-back trail that takes you to the largest natural sandstone arch in Sedona. The trail has some steep inclines and rocky terrain, but the views from the top are breathtaking.
2. **Schnebly Hill Trail**: A 5.5-mile out-and-back trail that offers panoramic views of Sedona's red rocks. The trail has some steep inclines and rocky terrain, but it's a great workout with rewarding views.
3. **Fay Canyon Trail**: A 2.5-mile out-and-back trail that takes you through a scenic canyon with towering red rock walls. The trail has some moderate to steep inclines, but the scenery is stunning.

**Safety Tips for Solo Hikers**

1. **Let someone know your itinerary**: Inform a friend or family member of your hiking plans, including the trail you'll be taking and your expected return time.
2. **Bring plenty of water and snacks**: Sedona's dry climate can be unforgiving, so make sure to stay hydrated and energized.
3. **Wear appropriate gear**: Wear sturdy hiking shoes, comfortable clothing, and a hat to protect yourself from the sun.
4. **Be mindful of the weather**: Sedona's weather can be unpredictable. Check the forecast before heading out, and be prepared for sudden changes in weather.
5. **Stay on marked trails**: Sedona's terrain can be challenging, and it's easy to get lost. Stick to marked trails and avoid taking unnecessary risks.

Remember to always prioritize your safety and well-being while hiking solo. Enjoy your time in Sedona, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions!
user: I'll definitely keep those safety tips in mind. I was thinking of staying in Sedona for a few days, and then heading to Monument Valley for a guided tour. Do you think that's a good amount of time to see both places, or should I plan for more days?
assistant: That sounds like a great plan! With a few days in Sedona and a guided tour in Monument Valley, you'll get a good taste of both destinations. However, considering the travel time between Sedona and Monument Valley, you might want to consider adding an extra day or two to your itinerary.

**Sedona**

Three days in Sedona is a good amount of time to:

* Explore the scenic trails and red rock formations
* Visit the art galleries and shops in Uptown Sedona
* Take a Jeep tour or wine tasting tour (if you're interested)
* Enjoy the stunning sunsets and sunrises

But, if you want to:

* Hike more trails or explore the surrounding areas (like Oak Creek Canyon or Cottonwood)
* Take a scenic drive to nearby attractions like Jerome or the Verde Canyon Railroad
* Simply relax and soak up the Sedona vibe

You might consider adding an extra day or two to your Sedona stay.

**Monument Valley**

A guided tour in Monument Valley is a great way to experience the Navajo Tribal Park's stunning scenery and learn about the Navajo culture. Most guided tours last around 2-4 hours, depending on the type of tour and the time of day.

If you want to:

* Take a sunrise or sunset tour for the best light
* Explore the surrounding areas, like the Four Corners Monument or the Navajo Code Talkers Museum
* Spend more time learning about the Navajo culture and history

You might consider adding an extra day to your Monument Valley stay. However, keep in mind that accommodations in Monument Valley are limited, and you might need to stay in nearby towns like Kayenta or Mexican Hat.

**Travel Time**

The drive from Sedona to Monument Valley takes around 4-5 hours, depending on traffic and the route you take. You'll want to factor in this travel time when planning your itinerary.

**Recommendation**

Considering the travel time and the amount of time you want to spend in each location, I'd recommend:

* 4-5 days in Sedona to explore the trails, scenic drives, and local attractions
* 2-3 days in Monument Valley for a guided tour and some extra time to explore the surrounding areas

Of course, this is just a suggestion. You can adjust the itinerary to fit your interests, travel style, and time constraints.

How does that sound?
user: I think that sounds like a good plan. I was also thinking about visiting the Hoover Dam on my way from Monument Valley back to Phoenix. Do you think that's doable, or would that be too much of a detour?
assistant: The Hoover Dam is an iconic landmark and a great addition to your trip.

The drive from Monument Valley to the Hoover Dam is around 4.5 hours, and then another 3.5 hours from the dam to Phoenix. While it's a bit of a detour, it's definitely doable, especially if you're interested in engineering, history, or just want to see another impressive man-made wonder.

Here's a rough outline of the drive:

* Monument Valley to Hoover Dam: 4.5 hours (via US-160 E and US-93 S)
* Visit the Hoover Dam (allow at least 2-3 hours for the tour and exploration)
* Hoover Dam to Phoenix: 3.5 hours (via US-93 S and I-10 W)

Keep in mind that you'll be driving through some remote areas, so make sure to:

* Fill up on gas and snacks before leaving Monument Valley
* Check the road conditions and weather forecast before heading out
* Bring sunscreen, water, and a hat to protect yourself from the desert sun

If you're short on time or prefer not to drive that much, you could consider skipping the Hoover Dam and heading straight back to Phoenix. However, if you're interested in seeing the dam and have the time, it's definitely worth the detour.

It's great that you're excited to explore the American Southwest on your own this time! Solo travel can be incredibly liberating, and you'll have the freedom to create your own itinerary and pace. Enjoy the independence and take in the stunning scenery around you!

How does that sound? Are you up for the Hoover Dam detour, or would you rather head straight back to Phoenix?
user: I think I'll take the detour to the Hoover Dam! It sounds like a great way to break up the drive and see another amazing landmark. Plus, I've always been fascinated by engineering and history, so I think I'd really enjoy the tour. Thanks for the advice and itinerary suggestions!
assistant: The Hoover Dam is an incredible feat of engineering and a fascinating piece of history. You'll get to see the dam's massive structure, learn about its construction, and even take a guided tour inside the dam. It's a great way to break up the drive and experience something unique.

I'm thrilled that I could help with your itinerary and provide you with some useful advice. If you have any more questions or need further recommendations, feel free to ask.

Remember to take your time, enjoy the scenic drives, and soak up the stunning views of the American Southwest. You're in for an amazing adventure!

Before I sign off, I just want to say that it's wonderful that you're taking the time to explore the Southwest on your own. Solo travel can be incredibly empowering, and I'm sure you'll return home with some amazing stories and memories.

Safe travels, and I hope you have an incredible time in Sedona, Monument Valley, and at the Hoover Dam!

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm trying to plan a trip to Arizona and I was wondering if you could recommend some must-see attractions and activities. By the way, I've been to the Grand Canyon before with my family on a road trip across the American Southwest a few years ago, but I'm looking to explore more of the state on my own this time.
assistant: Arizona has so much to offer beyond the Grand Canyon! I'd be happy to recommend some must-see attractions and activities for your solo trip. Here are a few suggestions:

**Nature and Outdoors**

1. **Sedona**: Known for its breathtaking red rock formations, Sedona is a hiker's paradise. Explore the scenic trails, take a Jeep tour, or simply soak up the vibrant energy of this artistic town.
2. **Monument Valley**: While you've seen the Grand Canyon, Monument Valley is another iconic Arizona landmark. Take a guided tour to explore the Navajo Tribal Park's stunning sandstone formations.
3. **Antelope Canyon**: A photographer's dream! This slot canyon near Page is a marvel of erosion, with surreal sandstone formations. Book a guided tour to explore its depths.
4. **Lake Powell**: Straddling the Arizona-Utah border, Lake Powell is a massive man-made lake with crystal-clear waters. Rent a boat, kayak, or take a guided tour to explore its many inlets and coves.

**Cities and Culture**

1. **Phoenix**: Arizona's capital city offers a rich cultural scene. Visit the Heard Museum to learn about Native American art and history, or explore the vibrant arts district in Roosevelt Row.
2. **Tucson**: This desert city is known for its Southwestern charm. Stroll through the historic adobe architecture of Downtown Tucson, visit the Arizona-Sonora Desert Museum, or explore the vibrant 4th Avenue arts district.
3. **Jerome**: This former mining town turned artist colony is perched on the side of Cleopatra Hill. Browse the shops, galleries, and restaurants, and enjoy the stunning views of the Verde Valley.

**Unique Experiences**

1. **Hot Air Expeditions**: Take a hot air balloon ride over the Sonoran Desert for a breathtaking sunrise view.
2. **Stargazing**: Arizona's clear skies make it an ideal spot for stargazing. Book a guided tour or visit one of the many dark sky preserves, such as the Oracle State Park.
3. **Native American Reservations**: Arizona is home to 22 Native American reservations. Visit the Navajo Nation, Hopi Tribe, or Tohono O'odham Nation to learn about their rich cultures and histories.

**Food and Drink**

1. **Southwestern Cuisine**: Try traditional dishes like tacos, burritos, and chimichangas at local eateries like Macayo's or BK Carne Asada.
2. **Craft Beer**: Arizona has a thriving craft beer scene. Visit breweries like Four Peaks Brewing Co. in Tempe or Borderlands Brewing Co. in Tucson.
3. **Prickly Pear Cactus Candy**: Indulge in this unique Arizona treat, made from the fruits of the prickly pear cactus.

These are just a few of the many amazing experiences Arizona has to offer. Depending on your interests and the time of year you visit, there are plenty of other attractions and activities to explore. Have a fantastic trip!
user: I'm really interested in exploring Sedona and Monument Valley, since I've already seen the Grand Canyon. Can you recommend any specific hiking trails in Sedona that are suitable for solo travelers?
assistant: Sedona and Monument Valley are both incredible destinations.

Sedona has numerous hiking trails for all skill levels, and many are suitable for solo travelers. Here are some popular and scenic trails to consider:

**Easy to Moderate Trails**

1. **Cathedral Rock Trail**: A 1.2-mile out-and-back trail that takes you to the base of Cathedral Rock, one of Sedona's most iconic formations. The trail is relatively flat and well-marked.
2. **Bell Rock Trail**: A 3.5-mile loop trail that offers stunning views of Bell Rock and the surrounding red rocks. The trail has some gentle inclines, but it's still accessible for most hikers.
3. **Boynton Canyon Trail**: A 6.1-mile out-and-back trail that takes you through a scenic canyon with towering red rock walls. The trail has some moderate inclines, but the scenery is well worth the effort.

**More Challenging Trails**

1. **Devil's Bridge Trail**: A 4.2-mile out-and-back trail that takes you to the largest natural sandstone arch in Sedona. The trail has some steep inclines and rocky terrain, but the views from the top are breathtaking.
2. **Schnebly Hill Trail**: A 5.5-mile out-and-back trail that offers panoramic views of Sedona's red rocks. The trail has some steep inclines and rocky terrain, but it's a great workout with rewarding views.
3. **Fay Canyon Trail**: A 2.5-mile out-and-back trail that takes you through a scenic canyon with towering red rock walls. The trail has some moderate to steep inclines, but the scenery is stunning.

**Safety Tips for Solo Hikers**

1. **Let someone know your itinerary**: Inform a friend or family member of your hiking plans, including the trail you'll be taking and your expected return time.
2. **Bring plenty of water and snacks**: Sedona's dry climate can be unforgiving, so make sure to stay hydrated and energized.
3. **Wear appropriate gear**: Wear sturdy hiking shoes, comfortable clothing, and a hat to protect yourself from the sun.
4. **Be mindful of the weather**: Sedona's weather can be unpredictable. Check the forecast before heading out, and be prepared for sudden changes in weather.
5. **Stay on marked trails**: Sedona's terrain can be challenging, and it's easy to get lost. Stick to marked trails and avoid taking unnecessary risks.

Remember to always prioritize your safety and well-being while hiking solo. Enjoy your time in Sedona, and don't hesitate to reach out if you have any more questions!
user: I'll definitely keep those safety tips in mind. I was thinking of staying in Sedona for a few days, and then heading to Monument Valley for a guided tour. Do you think that's a good amount of time to see both places, or should I plan for more days?
assistant: That sounds like a great plan! With a few days in Sedona and a guided tour in Monument Valley, you'll get a good taste of both destinations. However, considering the travel time between Sedona and Monument Valley, you might want to consider adding an extra day or two to your itinerary.

**Sedona**

Three days in Sedona is a good amount of time to:

* Explore the scenic trails and red rock formations
* Visit the art galleries and shops in Uptown Sedona
* Take a Jeep tour or wine tasting tour (if you're interested)
* Enjoy the stunning sunsets and sunrises

But, if you want to:

* Hike more trails or explore the surrounding areas (like Oak Creek Canyon or Cottonwood)
* Take a scenic drive to nearby attractions like Jerome or the Verde Canyon Railroad
* Simply relax and soak up the Sedona vibe

You might consider adding an extra day or two to your Sedona stay.

**Monument Valley**

A guided tour in Monument Valley is a great way to experience the Navajo Tribal Park's stunning scenery and learn about the Navajo culture. Most guided tours last around 2-4 hours, depending on the type of tour and the time of day.

If you want to:

* Take a sunrise or sunset tour for the best light
* Explore the surrounding areas, like the Four Corners Monument or the Navajo Code Talkers Museum
* Spend more time learning about the Navajo culture and history

You might consider adding an extra day to your Monument Valley stay. However, keep in mind that accommodations in Monument Valley are limited, and you might need to stay in nearby towns like Kayenta or Mexican Hat.

**Travel Time**

The drive from Sedona to Monument Valley takes around 4-5 hours, depending on traffic and the route you take. You'll want to factor in this travel time when planning your itinerary.

**Recommendation**

Considering the travel time and the amount of time you want to spend in each location, I'd recommend:

* 4-5 days in Sedona to explore the trails, scenic drives, and local attractions
* 2-3 days in Monument Valley for a guided tour and some extra time to explore the surrounding areas

Of course, this is just a suggestion. You can adjust the itinerary to fit your interests, travel style, and time constraints.

How does that sound?
user: I think that sounds like a good plan. I was also thinking about visiting the Hoover Dam on my way from Monument Valley back to Phoenix. Do you think that's doable, or would that be too much of a detour?
assistant: The Hoover Dam is an iconic landmark and a great addition to your trip.

The drive from Monument Valley to the Hoover Dam is around 4.5 hours, and then another 3.5 hours from the dam to Phoenix. While it's a bit of a detour, it's definitely doable, especially if you're interested in engineering, history, or just want to see another impressive man-made wonder.

Here's a rough outline of the drive:

* Monument Valley to Hoover Dam: 4.5 hours (via US-160 E and US-93 S)
* Visit the Hoover Dam (allow at least 2-3 hours for the tour and exploration)
* Hoover Dam to Phoenix: 3.5 hours (via US-93 S and I-10 W)

Keep in mind that you'll be driving through some remote areas, so make sure to:

* Fill up on gas and snacks before leaving Monument Valley
* Check the road conditions and weather forecast before heading out
* Bring sunscreen, water, and a hat to protect yourself from the desert sun

If you're short on time or prefer not to drive that much, you could consider skipping the Hoover Dam and heading straight back to Phoenix. However, if you're interested in seeing the dam and have the time, it's definitely worth the detour.

It's great that you're excited to explore the American Southwest on your own this time! Solo travel can be incredibly liberating, and you'll have the freedom to create your own itinerary and pace. Enjoy the independence and take in the stunning scenery around you!

How does that sound? Are you up for the Hoover Dam detour, or would you rather head straight back to Phoenix?
user: I think I'll take the detour to the Hoover Dam! It sounds like a great way to break up the drive and see another amazing landmark. Plus, I've always been fascinated by engineering and history, so I think I'd really enjoy the tour. Thanks for the advice and itinerary suggestions!
assistant: The Hoover Dam is an incredible feat of engineering and a fascinating piece of history. You'll get to see the dam's massive structure, learn about its construction, and even take a guided tour inside the dam. It's a great way to break up the drive and experience something unique.

I'm thrilled that I could help with your itinerary and provide you with some useful advice. If you have any more questions or need further recommendations, feel free to ask.

Remember to take your time, enjoy the scenic drives, and soak up the stunning views of the American Southwest. You're in for an amazing adventure!

Before I sign off, I just want to say that it's wonderful that you're taking the time to explore the Southwest on your own. Solo travel can be incredibly empowering, and I'm sure you'll return home with some amazing stories and memories.

Safe travels, and I hope you have an incredible time in Sedona, Monument Valley, and at the Hoover Dam!
